details_relating_to_any_risks_of_self_ha.more_details_thoughts_of_self_harm: Over the past three to four months I have been having recurring thoughts about harming myself, particularly during late evenings after work when I feel most overwhelmed and alone. These thoughts are mostly about wanting to escape constant feelings of worthlessness and exhaustion rather than a clear wish to die. I sometimes picture cutting my arms or driving my car into a wall, but I have not made any concrete plans or preparations. When these thoughts come up, they can last from a few minutes to an hour. They are more frequent when I have had a difficult day at work, arguments with my ex-partner about contact with our son, or when I have been drinking. I usually manage to distract myself by playing video games, going for a walk, or texting a friend, but it is becoming harder to shake the thoughts off. I have not shared the full extent of these thoughts with many people; my GP knows I feel low but I have tended to downplay the self-harm side because I am worried about stigma and how it might affect access to my son.
details_relating_to_any_risks_of_self_ha.more_details_self_harmed_in_last_6_month: Within the last 6 months I have engaged in self-harm on three occasions, all involving superficial cutting on my upper left thigh where it is not easily visible. The first incident was in early August 2025 after a particularly intense argument with my ex-partner over child maintenance and contact arrangements. I had been drinking that evening and felt completely hopeless and like a failure as a father. I used a new razor blade and made several shallow cuts; I did not need medical attention, but I did clean the cuts with antiseptic the next day. The second incident was in late September 2025 after a very stressful week at work where I was told I might be at risk of redundancy. I felt overwhelmed and like I had no control over anything, and cutting felt like a way to release some of that pressure. The third incident was in mid-October 2025 when I was feeling particularly low around the anniversary of my father’s death. In each case, the self-harm was impulsive and done when I was alone in my flat late at night. I have since thrown away blades and tried to avoid keeping anything sharp easily accessible, but the urges are still there at times.
details_relating_to_any_risks_of_self_ha.more_details_self_harmed_in_last_12_mont: In total, over the last 12 months I have self-harmed on around five occasions. The first two incidents happened late in 2024, shortly after my relationship with my ex-partner ended and I moved out of the family home into my current one-bedroom flat. Those earlier episodes also involved superficial cutting on my thighs using disposable razors. At that time I was drinking more heavily at weekends and using self-harm as a way to cope with intense feelings of guilt about the breakup and being away from my son. I did not seek medical help for any of these incidents, and I have not been admitted to hospital for self-harm. The frequency is not regular but tends to cluster around major stressors such as financial worries, conflict around co-parenting, or feeling isolated. I am increasingly worried that, if things continue as they are and I do not get proper support, the self-harm could escalate in severity or become more frequent.
details_relating_to_any_risks_of_self_ha.more_details_any_suicidal_behaviours_att: I have not made any actual suicide attempts in the past, and I have never taken an overdose, tried to hang myself, or done anything that required emergency medical treatment. However, I have come close to acting on suicidal thoughts on two occasions. Around March 2025, after receiving a letter about rent arrears, I sat in my car late at night in an empty car park and seriously thought about driving into a concrete barrier. I did not do it, but I stayed there for about an hour crying and imagining what it would be like. On another occasion in July 2025, I looked up information online about lethal doses of over-the-counter painkillers and considered buying a large quantity, but I did not follow through and closed the browser. I recognise these as warning signs that my thoughts can get quite dark, and that is part of the reason I agreed to be referred to JourneyMEN CIC. I want support before things escalate to an actual attempt.
details_relating_to_any_risks_of_self_ha.more_details_any_self_neglect: Over the last year I have gradually started neglecting my own basic needs, especially when my mood is low. On days when I am not working or when I am working from home, I often stay in bed until late morning or early afternoon, skipping breakfast and sometimes only eating one proper meal in the evening, usually takeaway or something processed like frozen pizzas. My flat has become noticeably cluttered, with unwashed dishes piling up and laundry left for weeks at a time. I sometimes go several days without showering or brushing my teeth, particularly after heavy drinking nights when I feel ashamed and unmotivated. Financially, I have ignored letters about utility bills and council tax arrears because I feel overwhelmed, which has led to late payment fees. I have missed two GP follow-up appointments for my mental health because I felt too anxious to leave the house and did not call to rearrange. Although I still manage to attend work most days, my self-care is poor outside of that, and friends have commented that I look run down and unkempt compared to how I used to be.

additional_potential_risks.brief_details_alcohol_misuse: Over the past two years my alcohol use has increased significantly, especially since the end of my long-term relationship in late 2024. I typically drink 4–6 evenings a week, mainly at home alone. On a standard night I will have 4–5 cans of strong lager (6% ABV) and sometimes a couple of double measures of whisky. At weekends, particularly when I do not have my son staying over, I can drink 8–10 cans in an evening. I rarely drink in pubs or bars anymore; most of my drinking is done in my flat while watching TV or gaming. I have had a few mornings where I have needed a drink first thing to steady my nerves, especially after very heavy sessions, although this is not yet a daily occurrence. My GP has told me I am drinking at a hazardous level and has advised cutting down, but I have struggled to do so because alcohol feels like the only way to switch off my thoughts and get to sleep. I have noticed that my mood is worse the day after drinking, I am more irritable, and my self-harm thoughts are stronger when I am hungover. I have also missed work twice this year due to being too hungover to attend, and my manager has informally raised concerns about my reliability.
additional_potential_risks.brief_details_drug_misuse: I do not currently use any illicit drugs and have never had a drug dependency. In my early twenties I experimented a few times with cannabis and cocaine at parties, but I stopped completely after my son was born and have not used any recreational drugs for over seven years. I am not prescribed any controlled drugs other than my antidepressant medication, which I take as directed.
additional_potential_risks.brief_details_self_harm_additional_poten: Self-harm is a current and ongoing risk for me. As detailed earlier, I have engaged in superficial cutting multiple times over the last 12 months, mainly on my thighs where it is hidden. The behaviour tends to occur when I feel overwhelmed, ashamed, or trapped, and it is often linked with alcohol use and arguments with my ex-partner. Although the injuries so far have not required medical treatment, I am concerned that in a particularly low moment I might cut deeper than intended or harm myself in a more dangerous way. I do not currently have a formal safety plan beyond trying to distract myself, messaging a friend, or going to bed when the urges are strong. I would benefit from structured support to develop healthier coping strategies and a clear crisis plan.
additional_potential_risks.brief_details_suicidal_thoughts: I experience suicidal thoughts several times a week, usually in the form of intrusive images of not being here anymore or scenarios where I die in an accident that I have staged. These thoughts are strongest late at night, when I am alone and ruminating about mistakes I feel I have made in my life, such as the breakdown of my relationship and not being the kind of father I wanted to be. The thoughts often include ideas like, "Everyone would be better off without me," or "My son will grow up hating me anyway." While I do think about methods (such as car crashes, overdoses, or jumping from a height), I have not made concrete plans like choosing a date or writing a note. The main protective factors that stop me from acting on these thoughts are my love for my 7-year-old son and the fear of leaving him without a father, as well as not wanting to hurt my mother and younger sister who have already been through the loss of my dad. However, the intensity and frequency of these thoughts worry me, and I am aware they could become more dangerous if my circumstances worsen or if I continue to feel unsupported.
additional_potential_risks.brief_details_suicide_attempts: I have never made a suicide attempt that required medical attention or intervention by emergency services. There have been a couple of episodes that I would describe as "near attempts" in that I came close to acting on my thoughts (e.g., sitting in my car in an isolated area late at night and seriously contemplating driving into a barrier), but I ultimately did not take any action beyond thinking and planning in my head. I have not stockpiled medication, written suicide notes, or carried out any acts with the clear intention of ending my life. My GP is aware that I have had suicidal thoughts but I have not always been fully open about how intense they can be, largely because I am afraid of being judged or losing access to my son.
additional_potential_risks.brief_details_mental_health: I have a history of anxiety and low mood going back to my late teens, but things have significantly worsened since around mid-2023. I was formally diagnosed with moderate to severe depression by my GP in February 2025 and have been taking sertraline since then. I experience persistent low mood, lack of motivation, feelings of hopelessness, and difficulty enjoying activities that I used to like, such as playing football and going out with friends. My sleep is poor; I either struggle to get to sleep or wake up in the early hours with racing thoughts, and I often feel exhausted during the day. I also experience anxiety symptoms including a tight chest, restlessness, and worrying excessively about work performance, finances, and contact with my son. Some days I find it hard to leave the flat due to feeling self-conscious and convinced everyone is judging me. These mental health difficulties affect my ability to concentrate at work, keep on top of household tasks, and maintain relationships. I have had a short course of telephone CBT through the NHS, which helped a bit with understanding my thought patterns, but I struggled to apply the techniques consistently without in-person support. I am seeking further support through JourneyMEN CIC because I feel I need a space specifically tailored to men to talk openly about my feelings, past experiences (including my father’s alcohol use and emotional absence), and how these are impacting my current life.
additional_potential_risks.brief_details_aggression_violence: I do not have a history of physical violence towards others, and I have never been arrested or charged with any violent offences. When I am under the influence of alcohol I can become more irritable and raise my voice during arguments, particularly with my ex-partner over the phone, but this has never escalated to physical aggression. I sometimes punch pillows or slam doors when I am extremely frustrated, but I have not damaged property or threatened anyone with harm. I am more likely to direct anger inwards at myself rather than outwards at others. I recognise that my tone and sharp words during conflict can still be hurtful, and I am interested in learning better ways to manage and express anger.
additional_potential_risks.brief_details_anti_social_behaviour: I am not involved in any anti-social behaviour. I do not associate with gangs, and I do not engage in activities such as vandalism, public disorder, or harassment. I keep mostly to myself in my flat, which has occasionally led to neighbours checking in because they rarely see me, but there have been no complaints about noise or behaviour. My only past legal issue was a parking fine that I paid late; I have never had a criminal record.
additional_potential_risks.brief_details_harassment_of_others: I have not harassed anyone and have no restraining orders or similar legal restrictions. My relationship with my ex-partner is strained, and we do argue over text and phone calls about arrangements for our son, but I make an effort to respect boundaries. If she asks for space, I back off, and I do not repeatedly call or message her. I am aware of how easily conflict can escalate and I am careful not to cross lines that could be considered harassment.
additional_potential_risks.brief_details_gambling_issues: I do not have any significant gambling issues. I occasionally buy a lottery ticket when the jackpot is very high or join a small low-stakes football accumulator with friends a few times a year, usually not more than £5–£10 at a time. I do not use online betting apps regularly, do not visit casinos, and gambling has never caused me financial hardship or conflict in relationships. My main financial difficulties are related to overspending on takeaways and alcohol, not gambling.
additional_potential_risks.brief_details_sexual_offender: I am not a sexual offender and have never been investigated, charged, or convicted of any sexual offence. There are no current or past restrictions on my contact with children beyond the informal arrangements agreed with my ex-partner regarding our son. I do not pose any known sexual risk to others.
additional_potential_risks.brief_details_victim_of_sexual_assault: I have not been the victim of sexual assault to my knowledge. While I have experienced some uncomfortable situations in the past, such as unwanted comments and minor boundary crossings when drunk in bars, there have been no incidents that I would describe as sexual assault. My main experiences of trauma are more related to emotional neglect and exposure to parental alcohol misuse during my childhood rather than sexual violence.
additional_potential_risks.brief_details_victim_of_sexual_offending: I have not been a victim of sexual offending. There are no ongoing legal cases or safeguarding issues relating to sexual abuse in my history. My difficulties stem more from long-term emotional stress, depression, and family dynamics than from sexual trauma.
additional_potential_risks.brief_details_adverse_childhood_experien: I grew up in a household where my father had significant alcohol problems and untreated depression. From around the age of 8, I remember him drinking heavily most evenings, becoming withdrawn and sometimes verbally harsh, calling me "useless" or "soft" when I cried. There were frequent arguments between my parents, often late at night, and I would lie awake listening, feeling scared they might split up or that something bad would happen. My mother worked long hours to keep the family afloat financially, so I often felt emotionally neglected and spent a lot of time alone or looking after my younger sister. There was no physical abuse, but the emotional atmosphere was tense and unpredictable. We did not talk about feelings, and I learned early on to keep things to myself and "just get on with it." My father died from complications related to his drinking when I was 23, which I found very hard to process because our relationship was unresolved. I also witnessed some domestic arguments where objects were thrown, which frightened me as a child. These experiences have left me with a strong fear of becoming like my dad and a tendency to bottle things up until they explode, contributing to my current mental health difficulties and relationship problems.

support_needs.brief_description_and_details_of_mental_:
I am currently struggling with ongoing symptoms of depression and anxiety that affect most areas of my life. My mood is low most days, and I often feel a heavy sense of guilt and failure, particularly about the breakdown of my relationship and not living with my son full time. I find it hard to get out of bed and start the day, especially on weekends when I do not have my son with me. I frequently cancel social plans at the last minute because I feel too drained or anxious to face people, which has led to me feeling increasingly isolated.

At work, I have difficulty concentrating and my mind often wanders to worries about money, my housing situation, and whether my ex-partner will reduce the time I can spend with our son. I work in a small warehouse and usually manage to get through the day by going on "autopilot," but I have made a few minor mistakes recently (e.g., mislabeling boxes, forgetting to log stock) which has knocked my confidence. I worry that if things continue like this I could lose my job, which would make my mental health even worse.

Emotionally, I often feel numb or detached, but then have intense bursts of sadness or anger, sometimes triggered by small things like seeing families together in the park or getting an unexpected bill. When these emotions hit, I find it hard to regulate myself and I sometimes turn to alcohol or self-harm to cope. I am also dealing with unresolved grief relating to my father’s death and the complicated feelings I have about his drinking and emotional absence.

In terms of support needs, I would benefit from:
- A safe, non-judgmental space to talk honestly about how I am feeling without worrying about burdening family or friends.
- Help developing healthier coping strategies for when I feel overwhelmed, such as techniques to manage urges to self-harm or drink.
- Support around building structure and routine into my days, including setting realistic goals for self-care, work, and parenting.
- Guidance on how to communicate better with my ex-partner about co-parenting, so that our interactions are less conflictual and I feel more confident as a father.
- Peer support from other men who have struggled with similar issues, so I do not feel so alone or ashamed.

I am motivated to engage with support because I want to be present and emotionally available for my son and to break the cycle of silence and alcohol use that I grew up with. I know I need help to do this and cannot manage it on my own.
support_needs.description_of_any_other_diagnosed_healt:
Aside from my diagnosed depression and anxiety, I have a couple of other health issues that impact me to a lesser extent. I have mild asthma, which was diagnosed in childhood. It is generally well controlled and mainly triggered by cold weather or chest infections. I use an inhaler as needed, but I have not had a serious asthma attack in many years.

I am also slightly overweight, with a BMI of around 29, which my GP has said puts me in the overweight category but not yet obese. My weight gain has been gradual over the last few years and seems linked to poor diet, lack of exercise, and increased alcohol intake. I occasionally get lower back pain, which I think is related to the physical nature of my job (lifting and moving boxes) and my posture. The back pain flares up if I do a lot of heavy lifting or if I have been particularly inactive and sitting for long periods. I have not been diagnosed with any serious physical conditions like diabetes or heart disease, but my GP has warned that my current lifestyle could increase my risk in future.

I do not have any known allergies apart from mild hay fever in the summer months, which I manage with over-the-counter antihistamines when needed.
support_needs.details_of_all_prescription_medications_:
I am currently prescribed the following medications:

1. Sertraline 100 mg tablets – I take one tablet once daily in the morning for depression and anxiety. I started on 50 mg in February 2025 and this was increased to 100 mg in May 2025 due to only partial improvement in symptoms. I have been mostly consistent with taking it, although I occasionally miss a dose when I am particularly disorganised or hungover.

2. Salbutamol (Ventolin) inhaler – 100 micrograms per puff, used as needed for asthma symptoms such as wheezing or shortness of breath. I usually only need it a few times a month, mainly when exercising in cold air or if I have a cough.

3. Over-the-counter pain relief – I sometimes take paracetamol 500 mg tablets, one or two tablets up to three times a day, for headaches or back pain. I do not take these daily, only when required, and I am aware of not exceeding the recommended maximum dose.

I am not currently on any sleep medication, although my GP has discussed the possibility of a short-term prescription if my insomnia continues. I am not prescribed any benzodiazepines or strong painkillers. I do not use any herbal remedies regularly, though I have occasionally tried things like chamomile tea or over-the-counter "sleep aid" tablets with mixed results.
support_needs.do_you_consider_yourself_to_be_disabled_:
I do not currently consider myself to be disabled, as I am still able to work and manage most day-to-day activities, albeit with difficulty at times. However, my mental health problems (depression and anxiety) do have a substantial impact on my ability to function, particularly in terms of motivation, concentration, and social interaction.

On bad days, my symptoms make it very hard to leave the house, attend appointments, or engage with other people. I sometimes call in sick to work because I feel completely drained and unable to cope with being around colleagues. I also find it challenging to manage household tasks like cleaning, cooking, and dealing with paperwork, which can build up and make me feel even more overwhelmed. Although I do not identify as disabled, I recognise that my mental health issues could be considered a form of hidden disability, and I would be open to discussing reasonable adjustments at work or in services to help me engage more fully (for example, flexibility around appointment times, the option for telephone or video check-ins when leaving the house feels too difficult, and clear written information about what to expect from any group sessions). I do not use any mobility aids and I do not have any formal disability status or benefits at present.
